Without controlled experiments (e.g., logistic regression without normalization, or a linear SVM), the claim that coincidence detection is responsible is unsupported.

comments.title: Discarding pre-training data contradicts original method
comments.quote: We discard the pre-training data, pre-process the training and test spectra by a range-1 mean filter, and use the default method for logistic regression in Wolfram Mathematica (L2-regularization = 0.0001, optimization method = limited-memory BFGS).
comments.explanation: The paper states that the original ResNet-26 was pre-trained on N×2000 spectra (60,000 spectra) before fine-tuning on the 3,000 training spectra. The proposed method discards this pre-training data entirely. This is not a fair comparison: the ResNet-26 baseline benefits from pre-training, while the logistic regression does not. If the logistic regression were also pre-trained on the same 60,000 spectra (e.g., via unsupervised feature learning or a larger logistic regression), the comparison would be more equitable. The paper should either include a version of logistic regression that uses the pre-training data (e.g., by training on the full 63,000 spectra) or explicitly discuss how the lack of pre-training affects the comparison.

comments.title: Mean filter is not defined or justified
comments.quote: pre-process the training and test spectra by a range-1 mean filter
comments.explanation: The term 'range-1 mean filter' is ambiguous. In signal processing, a mean filter typically replaces each value with the average of its neighbors within a window. 'Range-1' could mean a window of radius 1 (i.e., 3-point average) or a window of size 1 (no filtering). The paper does not specify the window size, the boundary handling, or whether the filter is applied to each spectrum independently. Moreover, no justification is given for why this preprocessing step is necessary or how it interacts with the subsequent z-score normalization. The authors should define the filter precisely (e.g., 'a moving average with a window of 3 adjacent wavelength bins, applied symmetrically with zero-padding at the boundaries') and explain its role in the method.

comments.title: Overclaiming and lack of limitations discussion
comments.quote: 2. Did you describe the limitations of your work? [Yes] Equation (1) makes it clear that we employ an approximation for coincidence detection.
comments.explanation: The abstract and introduction make broad claims about 'strengthening the case for revisiting classic methods' and imply that coincidence detection could beat deep learning more generally. Yet the paper acknowledges no limitations beyond the approximation in Eq. (1). There is no discussion of when the method might fail (e.g., with non-linear separability, high-dimensional data, or time-series inputs). The conclusion speculates about continual learning and neuromorphic hardware without any supporting experiments. A responsible paper would clearly state the scope and conditions under which the results hold.

comments.title: No discussion of computational efficiency or scalability
comments.quote: The coincidence detection (via normalized logistic regression) method introduced here achieves a test accuracy greater than ResNet-26 (see Table 1), and it took less than 3 seconds to train the classifier on a modern desktop (without any special-purpose GPUs).
comments.explanation: The paper mentions that training took 'less than 3 seconds on a modern desktop' but does not compare inference speed, memory usage, or scalability to larger datasets. For a method that claims to be a neuromorphic alternative to deep learning, it is essential to discuss energy efficiency, hardware compatibility (e.g., on Loihi), and whether the approach can handle high-dimensional inputs (e.g., images with millions of pixels). The current logistic regression on 1000-dimensional vectors is trivial and does not demonstrate the advantages of true spiking coincidence detection.

comments.title: Abstract contains undefined jargon and vague speculation
comments.quote: Hence, we cannot remain comfortably numb to the prevailing dogma that efficient matrix-vector operations is all we need; but should enquire with greater vigour if more advanced continual learning methods (running on spiking neural network hardware with neuromodulatory mechanisms at multiple timescales) can beat the accuracy of task-specific deep learning methods.
comments.explanation: This sentence is a rhetorical flourish that introduces undefined concepts ('continual learning methods', 'spiking neural network hardware', 'neuromodulatory mechanisms at multiple timescales') without any explanation or connection to the claimed coincidence detection method. The abstract should state what the paper actually does, not speculate about future research directions. The phrase 'cannot remain comfortably numb' is informal and inappropriate for a scientific abstract. Rewrite this sentence to state the paper's actual contribution and findings concretely.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Rosenblatt's Tobermory perceptron capabilities
comments.quote: the synaptic delays were not learnt but instead randomly distributed in Rosenblatt’s Tobermory perceptrons *[5]*, and this was rich enough to realize concentration-invariant and uniform time-warp invariant spatiotemporal classification by logarithmic encoding and coincidence detection.
comments.explanation: The claim that Rosenblatt's Tobermory perceptron realized 'concentration-invariant and uniform time-warp invariant spatiotemporal classification' is not supported by the cited reference [5] (Rosenblatt, 1961). Rosenblatt's Tobermory perceptron used random delays to achieve translation invariance in space and time, but 'concentration invariance' and 'uniform time-warp invariance' are not terms Rosenblatt used, nor did he demonstrate such properties. The paper's own later sections replace coincidence detection with logistic regression on z-scored inputs, which does not implement logarithmic encoding or time-warp invariance. This overstates what the historical method achieved. Rewrite as: 'the synaptic delays were not learnt but instead randomly distributed in Rosenblatt’s Tobermory perceptrons [5], enabling a form of spatiotemporal pattern recognition via coincidence detection.'

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper claims that a simple coincidence detection approximation, implemented as logistic regression on normalized data, outperforms a deep learning baseline (ResNet-26) on a bacterial classification task. While the result is numerically correct, the paper's framing as a breakthrough in neuromorphic computing is deeply misleading. The core 'coincidence detection' mechanism is mathematically reduced to standard inner products via normalization, rendering the neuromorphic framing superficial. The method is effectively a standard statistical classifier, and the comparison to ResNet-26 is apples-to-oranges due to fundamental differences in data preprocessing and feature engineering.

The paper presents a mathematically sound but conceptually weak result. The novelty lies entirely in the framing of a standard logistic regression as 'coincidence detection' via a loose approximation. The comparison to ResNet-26 is unfair because the ResNet model relies on complex feature extraction from raw spectra, while the proposed method uses a simple mean filter and normalization. The contribution is not a new neuromorphic algorithm but a re-interpretation of a standard linear classifier. The paper fails to demonstrate any advantage of the neuromorphic approach over standard ML, nor does it address the practical limitations of implementing 'coincidence detection' in hardware.

Overview issues:

- Misleading Framing of 'Coincidence Detection' as a Neuromorphic Breakthrough: The paper claims that coincidence detection, a classic neuromorphic mechanism, can compete with deep learning. However, the core approximation in Equation (1) reduces coincidence detection to a standard inner product between normalized vectors: $w_n 	op 	ilde{x}$. This is mathematically equivalent to a cosine similarity or a linear classifier with L2-normalized inputs. The 'neuromorphic' aspect—spike timing, coincidence, and distributed transmission times—is completely abstracted away by the normalization step. The paper essentially claims that a standard statistical classifier is a neuromorphic breakthrough. This framing misrepresents the contribution and confuses the reader regarding the potential of neuromorphic computing. The 'coincidence detection' operator $ igwedge$ is not actually used in the implementation; it is only invoked in the approximation. The paper would be much stronger if it focused on the approximation itself rather than the neuromorphic framing.

- Unfair and Incomparable Baseline Comparison: The paper compares its method to ResNet-26 on a bacterial classification task. However, the comparison is fundamentally flawed. The ResNet-26 model is trained on raw spectral data with complex feature extraction layers. In contrast, the proposed method applies a simple 'range-1 mean filter' and normalization. This preprocessing is so aggressive that it discards much of the high-frequency information that the ResNet is designed to capture. The 'coincidence detection' method is essentially a linear classifier on smoothed, normalized data. It is not surprising that it performs competitively; it is essentially a much simpler model that is being compared to a much more complex one. The paper needs to either (a) apply the same preprocessing to the ResNet-26 model to make the comparison fair, or (b) acknowledge that the comparison is between a simple, hand-crafted feature set and a deep feature set. The current framing implies that coincidence detection is a general-purpose competitor to deep learning, which is not supported by the evidence.

- Missing Critical Analysis of Approximation Validity: The paper introduces an approximation in Equation (1) to translate coincidence detection into a standard inner product. However, there is no theoretical or empirical analysis of the validity of this approximation. The paper states that coincidence detection depends on the specificities of ion-channels and membranes, but then simply assumes a translation and scale-invariant property. This is a significant leap of faith. The approximation implies that the timing of spikes is irrelevant and that only the magnitude of the signal matters, which is a fundamental departure from the biological and neuromorphic premise of the paper. The paper needs to provide a rigorous justification for this approximation or demonstrate its validity on the specific dataset. Without this, the core theoretical contribution is unsubstantiated.

- Lack of Practical Feasibility and Hardware Relevance: The paper is framed as a contribution to neuromorphic computing, but it provides no evidence that the proposed method is feasible or advantageous in a neuromorphic hardware context. The method is implemented on a standard desktop computer using logistic regression. The paper does not discuss the computational cost of implementing 'coincidence detection' in hardware, the energy efficiency, or the scalability of the approach. The 'coincidence detection' operator $ igwedge$ is not defined in a way that is implementable in hardware. The paper would be much stronger if it included a discussion of the practical implications of the approximation for neuromorphic hardware, or if it acknowledged that the current work is primarily a theoretical exercise with limited immediate hardware relevance.

- Missing demonstration that coincidence detection approximation holds: The paper claims coincidence detection is competitive with deep learning by approximating it with a standard inner product (Eq. 1), but it never demonstrates this approximation is valid for the specific dataset. Since the biological mechanism relies on spike timing and distributed delays, the claim that simple normalization preserves the classification logic is untested. You should simulate a simple spiking model where inputs are generated with random transmission delays and verify that the classification accuracy of the actual coincidence operator matches the accuracy of the normalized inner product approximation. This would establish that the simplification is not just mathematically convenient, but empirically justified for this problem.

- Missing comparison to linear baselines on raw data: The paper compares its method to a complex deep learning model (ResNet-26) but does not compare it to a simple linear classifier on the raw spectral data. This comparison is necessary to determine whether the performance gain comes from the 'coincidence detection' mechanism or simply from the preprocessing (range-1 mean filter and normalization). If a linear classifier on raw data performs similarly, it suggests the deep learning model is overfitting to the noise introduced by the preprocessing, rather than extracting meaningful features. You should train a standard linear SVM or logistic regression on the raw, unfiltered spectral data and report its accuracy alongside the existing results.

- Missing analysis of computational cost and hardware feasibility: The paper frames the contribution as a neuromorphic signal processing method, yet it provides no discussion of the computational cost or energy efficiency of implementing the proposed 'coincidence detection' operator in hardware. The current implementation on a desktop computer using logistic regression does not address the core premise of neuromorphic computing, which is energy efficiency and parallelism. You should estimate the number of operations required to compute the coincidence operator for a spike train and compare this to the number of operations required for standard matrix-vector multiplication. This analysis would clarify whether the method offers any practical advantages for hardware implementation.

Recommendation: Reject. The paper's core claim—that coincidence detection is a competitive alternative to deep learning—is not substantiated by the presented work. The method is effectively a standard logistic regression, and the comparison to ResNet-26 is unfair due to disparate preprocessing. The 'neuromorphic' framing is misleading and adds no value to the mathematical result. The paper would need a complete rewrite to focus on the approximation itself or to conduct a fair comparison with a properly preprocessed baseline.

Revision targets:

- Provide a rigorous theoretical justification or empirical validation for the approximation in Equation (1).

- Conduct a fair comparison by applying the same preprocessing (range-1 mean filter and normalization) to the ResNet-26 model.

- Clarify the 'coincidence detection' operator $ igwedge$ and discuss its practical implementation in neuromorphic hardware.

- Reframe the paper to focus on the approximation itself rather than the neuromorphic framing.

comments.title: Historical Accuracy of Tobermory Perceptron Capabilities
comments.quote: The propagating signals were binary (compatible with a spike-based view), the synaptic delays (transmission times) and weights (memory states) could be analog, the network could be recurrent and was often randomly interconnected, and learning often meant tuning the weights of the association-receptor subnetwork by some error-corrective reinforcement. The synaptic delays were not learnt but instead randomly distributed in Rosenblatt’s Tobermory perceptrons *[5]*, and this was rich enough to realize concentration-invariant and uniform time-warp invariant spatiotemporal classification by logarithmic encoding and coincidence detection.
comments.explanation: The introduction attributes 'uniform time-warp invariant' capabilities to Rosenblatt’s Tobermory perceptrons. However, the original 1958 paper describes the perceptron as capable of 'concentration-invariant' classification but does not describe 'time-warp invariant' capabilities. The perceptron is a feed-forward network with fixed weights and no mechanism to handle temporal warping or dynamic time alignment. The claim that Tobermory perceptrons realized 'uniform time-warp invariant' classification is a significant overstatement of the original model's capabilities. The authors should revise this sentence to accurately reflect the limitations of the original perceptron model.

comments.title: Unjustified Approximation of Coincidence Detection
comments.quote: We do not yet have a complete theory of neuromorphic signal processing, so here we introduce an approximation for the translation and scale-invariant property of coincidence detection as
comments.explanation: Equation (1) replaces the biological coincidence operator $ igwedge$ with a standard dot product on normalized vectors. The paper acknowledges this is an approximation, but it offers no theoretical or empirical justification for why this simplification is valid for the specific biological mechanism or the Raman spectroscopy data. The claim that coincidence detection is 'translation and scale-invariant' contradicts the biological premise that relies on spike timing and distributed delays. To substantiate the core theoretical contribution, the authors must either derive the conditions for this approximation or demonstrate empirically that the normalized dot product yields the same classification accuracy as the actual coincidence operator.

comments.title: Unfair Baseline Comparison Due to Aggressive Preprocessing
comments.quote: We discard the pre-training data, pre-process the training and test spectra by a range-1 mean filter, and use the default method for logistic regression in Wolfram Mathematica (L2-regularization = 0.0001, optimization method = limited-memory BFGS).
comments.explanation: The comparison between the proposed method and ResNet-26 is fundamentally flawed. The proposed method applies a 'range-1 mean filter' and normalization to the spectral data, which aggressively discards high-frequency information. In contrast, ResNet-26 is trained on raw spectral data. This preprocessing step fundamentally alters the data distribution, meaning the performance difference cannot be attributed to the 'coincidence detection' mechanism but rather to the smoothing. The authors should either apply the same preprocessing to the ResNet-26 model to ensure a fair comparison or explicitly acknowledge that they are comparing a smoothed signal to a raw signal.

comments.title: Misleading Notation in Accuracy Table
comments.explanation: The table lists ResNet-26 accuracy as 82.2±0.3, while the introduction cites a flat 82.2%. This notation is misleading because the ±0.3 suggests a statistical measure that is not present in the source [10]. The authors should align the presentation by removing the standard deviation notation from the table entry to avoid confusion.
